Hash Map Cheatsheet

Overview

Hash Map (Hash Table/Dictionary) is a fundamental data structure that provides efficient key-value storage and retrieval operations.

Key Properties

  • Average Time Complexity: O(1) for insert, delete, and search
  • Worst Time Complexity: O(n) for insert, delete, and search (when all keys hash to same bucket)
  • Space Complexity: O(n)
  • Implementation: Array + Linked List/Red-Black Tree (Java HashMap)
  • Hash Collisions: Handled via chaining or open addressing

When Hash Collisions Occur

  • Load Factor > 0.75: Performance degrades
  • Poor Hash Function: Many keys map to same bucket
  • Java HashMap: Converts linked list to red-black tree when length > 8

  • NC - HashMap under the hood

Problem Categories

1. Counting and Frequency

Description: Track frequency of elements, characters, or patterns. Key Insight: Use hash map as counter to avoid nested loops. Examples:

  • Character frequency in strings
  • Element counting in arrays
  • Anagram detection
  • Most frequent elements

2. Two Sum Variants and Complement Finding

Description: Find pairs, triplets, or complements that satisfy specific conditions. Key Insight: Store elements and check for required complement. Examples:

  • Two Sum, Three Sum, Four Sum
  • Pair differences (k-diff pairs)
  • Subarray sum problems
  • Target sum combinations

3. Prefix Sum and Subarray Problems

Description: Use cumulative sums with hash map to find subarrays with target properties. Key Insight: subarray[i,j] = prefixSum[j] - prefixSum[i-1] Examples:

  • Subarray sum equals K
  • Continuous subarray sum
  • Maximum size subarray sum equals K
  • Binary array with equal 0s and 1s

4. Sliding Window with Hash Map

Description: Maintain a dynamic window while tracking element frequency or properties. Key Insight: Hash map maintains window state efficiently. Examples:

  • Longest substring without repeating characters
  • Minimum window substring
  • Find all anagrams in string
  • Permutation in string

5. Design and Caching

Description: Implement data structures or caching mechanisms using hash maps. Key Insight: Hash map provides O(1) access for cache operations. Examples:

  • LRU Cache
  • LFU Cache
  • Design HashMap
  • Design HashSet

6. Graph and Tree Problems with Hash Map

Description: Use hash map to store graph relationships, tree paths, or node mappings. Key Insight: Hash map simplifies complex relationship tracking. Examples:

  • Clone graph
  • Tree serialization/deserialization
  • Find duplicate subtrees
  • Lowest common ancestor with parent pointers

Templates and Patterns

Template 1: Counting/Frequency Pattern

1# Universal Counting Template
2def counting_pattern(arr):
3    count = {}  # or collections.defaultdict(int)
4    result = []
5    
6    # Count frequency
7    for item in arr:
8        count[item] = count.get(item, 0) + 1
9        # or count[item] += 1 with defaultdict
10    
11    # Process based on frequency
12    for key, freq in count.items():
13        if meets_condition(freq):
14            result.append(key)
15    
16    return result
17
18# Examples: LC 49, LC 242, LC 451, LC 347, LC 692

Template 2: Two Sum/Complement Finding

1# Two Sum Pattern Template
2def two_sum_pattern(nums, target):
3    seen = {}  # {value: index}
4    
5    for i, num in enumerate(nums):
6        complement = target - num
7        
8        if complement in seen:
9            return [seen[complement], i]
10        
11        seen[num] = i
12    
13    return []
14
15# Variations:
16# - Multiple pairs: collect all instead of returning first
17# - K-diff pairs: check for num+k and num-k
18# - Examples: LC 1, LC 167, LC 15, LC 532, LC 1010

Template 3: Prefix Sum with Hash Map

See detailed pattern explanation in 0-2) Pattern > Subarray Sum Count Pattern

1# Prefix Sum Pattern Template
2def prefix_sum_pattern(nums, target):
3    prefix_sum = 0
4    sum_count = {0: 1}  # {sum: count/index}
5    result = 0
6
7    for num in nums:
8        prefix_sum += num
9
10        # Check if (prefix_sum - target) exists
11        if prefix_sum - target in sum_count:
12            result += sum_count[prefix_sum - target]
13
14        # Update current prefix sum count
15        sum_count[prefix_sum] = sum_count.get(prefix_sum, 0) + 1
16
17    return result
18
19# For max length problems, store index instead of count:
20# sum_index = {0: -1}, then calculate i - sum_index[prefix_sum - target]
21# Examples: LC 560, LC 325, LC 525, LC 523

Key Differences by Problem Type:

  • Count problems (LC 560, 930, 974): Store {sum: count}, check then update
    • LC 974 variant: Use modulo {remainder: count}, MUST handle negative remainders!
  • Max length problems (LC 325, 525): Store {sum: first_index}, only update if new
    • LC 525 variant: Transform problem (0→-1, 1→+1), initialize with {0: -1}, store first occurrence only
  • Existence problems (LC 523): Store {sum: any_index}, just need to find one

Template 4: Sliding Window with Hash Map

1# Sliding Window with HashMap Template
2def sliding_window_hashmap(s, pattern):
3    if len(pattern) > len(s):
4        return []
5    
6    pattern_count = {}
7    window_count = {}
8    
9    # Count pattern frequency
10    for char in pattern:
11        pattern_count[char] = pattern_count.get(char, 0) + 1
12    
13    left = 0
14    result = []
15    
16    for right in range(len(s)):
17        # Expand window
18        char = s[right]
19        window_count[char] = window_count.get(char, 0) + 1
20        
21        # Contract window if needed
22        while window_size_condition_met():
23            # Check if current window is valid
24            if window_count == pattern_count:
25                result.append(left)
26            
27            # Remove leftmost character
28            left_char = s[left]
29            window_count[left_char] -= 1
30            if window_count[left_char] == 0:
31                del window_count[left_char]
32            left += 1
33    
34    return result
35
36# Examples: LC 3, LC 76, LC 438, LC 567

Template 5: Hash Map for Caching/Memoization

1# Caching/Memoization Template
2class CacheTemplate:
3    def __init__(self, capacity):
4        self.capacity = capacity
5        self.cache = {}  # key -> value
6        self.usage = {}  # key -> usage_info
7    
8    def get(self, key):
9        if key in self.cache:
10            self.update_usage(key)
11            return self.cache[key]
12        return -1
13    
14    def put(self, key, value):
15        if len(self.cache) >= self.capacity:
16            self.evict()
17        
18        self.cache[key] = value
19        self.update_usage(key)
20    
21    def update_usage(self, key):
22        # Update usage tracking
23        pass
24    
25    def evict(self):
26        # Remove least recently/frequently used
27        pass
28
29# Examples: LC 146 (LRU), LC 460 (LFU)

Template 6: Graph Problems with Hash Map

1# Graph with HashMap Template
2def graph_hashmap_pattern(graph_input):
3    # Build adjacency list/map
4    graph = {}  # node -> [neighbors] or node -> {neighbor: weight}
5    
6    for edge in graph_input:
7        node1, node2 = edge[0], edge[1]
8        if node1 not in graph:
9            graph[node1] = []
10        if node2 not in graph:
11            graph[node2] = []
12        
13        graph[node1].append(node2)
14        graph[node2].append(node1)  # for undirected
15    
16    # Process using DFS/BFS with visited tracking
17    visited = set()
18    result = []
19    
20    def dfs(node):
21        if node in visited:
22            return
23        
24        visited.add(node)
25        result.append(node)
26        
27        for neighbor in graph.get(node, []):
28            dfs(neighbor)
29    
30    return result
31
32# Examples: LC 133, LC 200, LC 694, LC 1257

Template 7: TreeMap Pattern (Ordered Map)

1# Python - SortedDict (from sortedcontainers)
2from sortedcontainers import SortedDict
3
4# TreeMap Pattern Template
5def treemap_pattern(data):
6    # TreeMap maintains sorted order by key
7    tree_map = SortedDict()
8
9    # Basic operations
10    tree_map[key] = value           # O(log n) insert
11    value = tree_map.get(key)       # O(log n) search
12    del tree_map[key]               # O(log n) delete
13
14    # Ordered operations
15    first_key = tree_map.keys()[0] if tree_map else None    # Min key
16    last_key = tree_map.keys()[-1] if tree_map else None    # Max key
17
18    # Range queries
19    # Get all keys in sorted order
20    for key in tree_map.keys():
21        process(key, tree_map[key])
22
23    # Find floor/ceiling (closest keys)
24    # bisect_left/bisect_right for approximate match
25    import bisect
26    keys = list(tree_map.keys())
27    idx = bisect.bisect_left(keys, target)  # Floor index
28
29    return tree_map
30
31# Examples: LC 853, LC 729/731/732, LC 846, LC 352, LC 981

1// Java - TreeMap Pattern
2import java.util.*;
3
4// TreeMap Pattern Template
5public void treeMapPattern(int[] data) {
6    // TreeMap maintains sorted order by key (Red-Black Tree)
7    TreeMap<Integer, Integer> treeMap = new TreeMap<>();
8
9    // Basic operations - O(log n)
10    treeMap.put(key, value);        // Insert
11    Integer value = treeMap.get(key);  // Search
12    treeMap.remove(key);            // Delete
13
14    // Ordered operations - O(log n)
15    Integer firstKey = treeMap.firstKey();   // Min key
16    Integer lastKey = treeMap.lastKey();     // Max key
17    Integer floorKey = treeMap.floorKey(k);  // Largest key <= k
18    Integer ceilKey = treeMap.ceilingKey(k); // Smallest key >= k
19
20    // Lower/Higher (exclusive)
21    Integer lower = treeMap.lowerKey(k);     // Largest key < k
22    Integer higher = treeMap.higherKey(k);   // Smallest key > k
23
24    // Range queries - O(k log n) where k is range size
25    Map.Entry<Integer, Integer> firstEntry = treeMap.firstEntry();
26    Map.Entry<Integer, Integer> lastEntry = treeMap.lastEntry();
27
28    // Iterate in sorted order - O(n)
29    for (Map.Entry<Integer, Integer> entry : treeMap.entrySet()) {
30        int key = entry.getKey();
31        int val = entry.getValue();
32        // Process in sorted order
33    }
34
35    // SubMap views (range queries)
36    SortedMap<Integer, Integer> subMap = treeMap.subMap(fromKey, toKey);
37    SortedMap<Integer, Integer> headMap = treeMap.headMap(toKey);
38    SortedMap<Integer, Integer> tailMap = treeMap.tailMap(fromKey);
39}

TreeMap vs HashMap Comparison:

Feature HashMap TreeMap
Ordering No ordering Sorted by key
Underlying Structure Hash Table + Linked List/Red-Black Tree (collision) Red-Black Tree
Insert/Delete/Search O(1) average, O(n) worst O(log n)
Iteration No specific order Sorted order by key
Floor/Ceiling Not supported O(log n)
Range Queries Not supported O(k log n)
Use Case Fast lookups, no ordering needed Ordered iteration, range queries, floor/ceiling
Memory Less (hash table) More (tree nodes + pointers)

When to Use TreeMap:

  • Need keys in sorted order
  • Need floor/ceiling operations (closest key)
  • Need range queries (all keys in [a, b])
  • Need first/last key efficiently
  • Problems involving intervals, ranges, or ordering constraints

When NOT to Use TreeMap:

  • Only need fast O(1) lookups without ordering
  • Memory is constrained (TreeMap uses more memory)
  • Don’t need ordered operations (HashMap is faster)

Common TreeMap Patterns:

  1. Pattern 1: Ordered Map for Sorting

    1// LC 853 - Car Fleet
2// Convert HashMap to TreeMap for sorted iteration
3Map<Integer, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
4// ... populate map ...
5TreeMap<Integer, Integer> sorted = new TreeMap<>(map);

  2. Pattern 2: Interval Management

    1// LC 729/731/732 - My Calendar series
2// Use TreeMap to check overlapping intervals
3TreeMap<Integer, Integer> calendar = new TreeMap<>();
4
5public boolean book(int start, int end) {
6    Integer prev = calendar.floorKey(start);
7    Integer next = calendar.ceilingKey(start);
8
9    if ((prev == null || calendar.get(prev) <= start) &&
10        (next == null || end <= next)) {
11        calendar.put(start, end);
12        return true;
13    }
14    return false;
15}

  3. Pattern 3: Consecutive Elements

    1// LC 846 - Hand of Straights
2// Use TreeMap to process smallest elements first
3TreeMap<Integer, Integer> count = new TreeMap<>();
4// ... count frequency ...
5
6while (!count.isEmpty()) {
7    int first = count.firstKey();
8    // Process consecutive sequence starting from first
9}

  4. Pattern 4: Range/Stream Problems

    1// LC 352 - Data Stream as Disjoint Intervals
2// Maintain disjoint intervals in sorted order
3TreeMap<Integer, int[]> intervals = new TreeMap<>();
4
5public void addNum(int val) {
6    Integer lower = intervals.floorKey(val);
7    Integer higher = intervals.ceilingKey(val);
8    // Merge intervals if needed
9}

Classic LeetCode Problems:

Problem LC# Difficulty Key TreeMap Operation
Car Fleet 853 Medium Sort by position (key)
My Calendar I 729 Medium floorKey/ceilingKey for overlap check
My Calendar II 731 Medium Count overlapping bookings
My Calendar III 732 Hard Maximum overlapping count
Hand of Straights 846 Medium firstKey for smallest element
Data Stream as Disjoint Intervals 352 Hard Merge intervals with floor/ceiling
Time Based Key-Value Store 981 Medium floorKey for timestamp lookup
Count of Smaller Numbers After Self 315 Hard Ordered iteration
Contains Duplicate III 220 Medium floorKey/ceilingKey for range check
The Skyline Problem 218 Hard Multiset with TreeMap

Example: LC 853 - Car Fleet

1# Python - LC 853 Car Fleet
2def carFleet(target, position, speed):
3    # Use sorted iteration (similar to TreeMap)
4    cars = sorted(zip(position, speed), reverse=True)  # Sort by position descending
5
6    stack = []
7    for pos, spd in cars:
8        time = (target - pos) / spd  # Time to reach target
9        if not stack or time > stack[-1]:
10            stack.append(time)
11
12    return len(stack)
13
14# Alternative using SortedDict
15from sortedcontainers import SortedDict
16
17def carFleet_v2(target, position, speed):
18    car_map = SortedDict()
19    for p, s in zip(position, speed):
20        car_map[-p] = s  # Negative for reverse order
21
22    fleets = 0
23    prev_time = 0
24
25    for neg_pos, spd in car_map.items():
26        pos = -neg_pos
27        time = (target - pos) / spd
28        if time > prev_time:
29            fleets += 1
30            prev_time = time
31
32    return fleets

1// Java - LC 853 Car Fleet
2/**
3 * time = O(N log N)
4 * space = O(N)
5 */
6public int carFleet(int target, int[] position, int[] speed) {
7    // Build HashMap first
8    Map<Integer, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
9    for (int i = 0; i < position.length; i++) {
10        map.put(position[i], speed[i]);
11    }
12
13    // Convert to TreeMap for sorted iteration (descending order)
14    TreeMap<Integer, Integer> treeMap = new TreeMap<>(Collections.reverseOrder());
15    treeMap.putAll(map);
16
17    int fleets = 0;
18    double prevTime = 0;
19
20    // Iterate from position closest to target (sorted order)
21    for (Map.Entry<Integer, Integer> entry : treeMap.entrySet()) {
22        int pos = entry.getKey();
23        int spd = entry.getValue();
24        double time = (double)(target - pos) / spd;
25
26        // If current car takes longer, it forms a new fleet
27        if (time > prevTime) {
28            fleets++;
29            prevTime = time;
30        }
31    }
32
33    return fleets;
34}

Example: LC 729 - My Calendar I

1// Java - LC 729 My Calendar I
2/**
3 * time = O(log N) per operation
4 * space = O(N)
5 */
6class MyCalendar {
7    TreeMap<Integer, Integer> calendar;
8
9    public MyCalendar() {
10        calendar = new TreeMap<>();
11    }
12
13    public boolean book(int start, int end) {
14        // Find largest start time <= current start
15        Integer prev = calendar.floorKey(start);
16
17        // Find smallest start time >= current start
18        Integer next = calendar.ceilingKey(start);
19
20        // Check no overlap with previous booking
21        if (prev != null && calendar.get(prev) > start) {
22            return false;
23        }
24
25        // Check no overlap with next booking
26        if (next != null && next < end) {
27            return false;
28        }
29
30        calendar.put(start, end);
31        return true;
32    }
33}

1# Python - LC 729 My Calendar I
2from sortedcontainers import SortedDict
3
4class MyCalendar:
5    """
6    time = O(log N) per operation
7    space = O(N)
8    """
9    def __init__(self):
10        self.calendar = SortedDict()
11
12    def book(self, start: int, end: int) -> bool:
13        # Find previous and next bookings
14        idx = self.calendar.bisect_left(start)
15
16        # Check previous booking
17        if idx > 0:
18            prev_start = self.calendar.keys()[idx - 1]
19            if self.calendar[prev_start] > start:
20                return False
21
22        # Check next booking
23        if idx < len(self.calendar):
24            next_start = self.calendar.keys()[idx]
25            if next_start < end:
26                return False
27
28        self.calendar[start] = end
29        return True

Interview Tips for TreeMap Problems:

  1. Recognition Patterns:

    • “sorted order”, “smallest/largest”, “floor/ceiling” → Think TreeMap
    • “overlapping intervals” → TreeMap with floorKey/ceilingKey
    • “consecutive elements” → TreeMap.firstKey() for greedy processing
    • “range queries” → TreeMap.subMap()

  2. Common Mistakes:

    • Forgetting O(log n) complexity vs O(1) for HashMap
    • Not handling null returns from floor/ceiling operations
    • Using TreeMap when HashMap would suffice
    • Not considering memory overhead of tree structure

  3. Optimization:

    • If only need sorted iteration once, sort array instead (O(n log n) vs maintaining TreeMap)
    • If range queries are rare, consider lazy sorting
    • For Python, sortedcontainers library provides efficient SortedDict

  4. Edge Cases:

    • Empty TreeMap (firstKey/lastKey throw exceptions)
    • Null values from floor/ceiling operations
    • Duplicate keys (TreeMap doesn’t allow, use value as counter)
    • Reverse order iteration (use descendingMap() in Java)

0) Concept

  • Java HashMap

    • Low level : Array + Linked list / red-black tree
      • if Linked list length > 8 -> transform Linked list to red-black tree
      • if Linked list length < 6 -> transform red-black tree back to Linked list

  • FAQ

    • why hashmap search time complexity ~= O(1) ? explain ?
      • TL;DR : O(1) is avg and best case. worst case could be O(N) (hash collision)
      • hash func matters -> how to storage data & possible hash collision happens
      • OP
        • insert
          • get key, get hash val via hash func
          • find bucket in memory based on hash val
          • save key and value in the bucket
        • query
          • get index based on key
          • find bucket location based on index
            • NOTE !!! use bit op (int pos = (n - 1) & hash), so this op can be O(1) time complexity. (find bucket address directly, NO need to loop over all items)
          • loop over all elements under that key (if there is one element, then do once)
          • return value

      • ref 1
      • ref 2

  • LC Ref

    • prefix_sum.md

0-1) Types

  • N sum:

    • n_sum.md

  • Prefix problems

    • prefix_sum.md
    • Continous sum
      • LC 525 : Contiguous Array ⭐ See detailed pattern
        • Transform + Prefix Sum: 0→-1, 1→+1, store {count: first_index}
      • LC 523 : Continuous Subarray Sum
    • Pair of sums
      • LC 1010 : Pairs of Songs With Total Durations Divisible by 60
    • Sub array sum
      • LC 560 : Subarray Sum Equals K
        • TODO : note this as pattern!!!
      • LC 325: Maximum Size Subarray Sum Equals k
        • prefix sum + hashmap
      • LC 974: Subarray Sums Divisible by K ⭐ See detailed pattern
        • Prefix Sum + Modulo: {remainder: count}, handle negative remainders!
      1subarray[i,j] = prefixSum[j] - prefixSum[i-1]
2
3so, to find a subarray equals k
4
5-> prefixSum[j] - prefixSum[i-1] = k
6
7-> prefixSum[j]  - k = prefixSum[i-1]
8
9-> so all need to do is : check if "prefixSum[j]  - k" is in map
    • check permutaion sub string
      • LC 567
      1// LC 567
2// ...
3     /** NOTE !!!
4     *
5     *  we use below trick to
6     *
7     *  -> 1) check if `new reached s2 val` is in s1 map
8     *  -> 2) check if 2 map are equal
9     *
10     *  -> so we have more simple code, and clean logic
11     */
12    if (map2.equals(map1)) {
13        return true;
14    }
15// ...

  • Check with letest existed idx

    • LC 763 Partition Labels

  • Top k element (with PQ)

    • LC 347, 215, 692

  • Any problems with below

    • need to cache
    • avoid double loop

0-2) Pattern

Subarray Sum Count Pattern (LC 560 Core Pattern)

Core Concept: Use hashmap to count ALL subarray combinations that sum to target in O(N) time with single loop.

Key Insight:

1If we want subarray[i,j] to sum to k:
2  presum[j] - presum[i-1] = k
3  → presum[i-1] = presum[j] - k
4
5So at index j, check if (presum[j] - k) exists in map!

Critical Implementation Details:

  1. Use Count, NOT Index:

    1Map<Integer, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();  // {prefixSum: count}
    • Same prefix sum can occur MULTIPLE times
    • We need to count ALL valid subarrays, not just find one
    • Example: [1, -1, 1, -1] with k=0 has multiple solutions

  2. Initialize with map.put(0, 1):

    1map.put(0, 1);  // Handle subarrays starting from index 0
    • When presum[j] == k, then presum[j] - k = 0
    • Need to count these subarrays starting from beginning

  3. Check BEFORE Update (Critical Order):

    1for (int num : nums) {
2    presum += num;
3
4    // 1. CHECK first: count how many previous prefix sums = (presum - k)
5    if (map.containsKey(presum - k)) {
6        count += map.get(presum - k);  // Add ALL occurrences
7    }
8
9    // 2. UPDATE after: add current prefix sum for future iterations
10    map.put(presum, map.getOrDefault(presum, 0) + 1);
11}
    • Why this order? Prevents counting current subarray with itself
    • Current prefix sum should only be available for FUTURE iterations

Why This Pattern Gets ALL Combinations:

  • Map stores ALL previously seen prefix sums with their counts
  • When we check presum - k, we get count of ALL previous occurrences
  • Each previous occurrence represents a valid starting point
  • count += map.get(presum - k) adds ALL valid subarrays ending at current index

Example Walkthrough (nums = [1,1,1], k = 2):

1i=0: num=1, presum=1
2  - Check: (1-2)=-1 not in map → count=0
3  - Update: map={0:1, 1:1}
4
5i=1: num=1, presum=2
6  - Check: (2-2)=0 in map, count += map[0] = 1 → count=1
7  - Update: map={0:1, 1:1, 2:1}
8
9i=2: num=1, presum=3
10  - Check: (3-2)=1 in map, count += map[1] = 1 → count=2
11  - Update: map={0:1, 1:1, 2:1, 3:1}
12
13Result: count=2 (subarrays [1,1] and [1,1])

Related LC Problems (Same Pattern):

  • LC 560: Subarray Sum Equals K (exact pattern)
  • LC 325: Maximum Size Subarray Sum Equals k (store index instead of count)
  • LC 930: Binary Subarrays with Sum
  • LC 974: Subarray Sums Divisible by K (use modulo {remainder: count}, handle negatives!)

Java Implementation Template:

1public int subarraySum(int[] nums, int k) {
2    // Map: {prefixSum: count} - NOT {prefixSum: index}!
3    Map<Integer, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
4    map.put(0, 1);  // Handle subarrays from index 0
5
6    int presum = 0;
7    int count = 0;
8
9    for (int num : nums) {
10        presum += num;
11
12        // Check if (presum - k) exists: presum - x = k → x = presum - k
13        if (map.containsKey(presum - k)) {
14            count += map.get(presum - k);  // Add ALL occurrences
15        }
16
17        // Update map AFTER checking (critical order!)
18        map.put(presum, map.getOrDefault(presum, 0) + 1);
19    }
20
21    return count;
22}

When to Use Count vs Index:

Problem Type Map Value Example Special Notes
Count ALL subarrays count LC 560, 930, 974 Check before update
Count (with modulo) count LC 974 Use remainder as key; handle negatives!
Find LONGEST subarray index (first occurrence) LC 325, 525 Store only first occurrence
Find LONGEST (with transformation) index (first occurrence) LC 525 Transform 0→-1, 1→+1; init {0:-1}
Find if EXISTS boolean/index LC 523 Any occurrence works

Common Mistakes:

  1. ❌ Using {prefixSum: index} for counting problems
  2. ❌ Updating map before checking (causes self-counting)
  3. ❌ Forgetting map.put(0, 1) initialization
  4. ❌ Not handling the case where prefix sum itself equals k
  5. ❌ [LC 974] Forgetting to handle negative remainders (Java/Python -7 % 5 = -2, need to add k to get 3)

1) General form

  • Definition

  • When to use

    • Use case that need data IO with ~ O(1) time complexity
    • optimization via cache (space - time tradeoff)
    • sum, pair, continuous
    • avoid double loop (O(N^2))

  • When Not to use

    • When data is time sequence
    • When data is in ordering
    • https://www.reddit.com/r/learnprogramming/comments/29t4s4/when_is_it_bad_to_use_a_hash_table/

  • Hash Collisions

    • Chaining
    • Open addressing
    • hash_map_collisions.md

  • Ref

    • https://blog.techbridge.cc/2017/01/21/simple-hash-table-intro/
    • https://www.freecodecamp.org/news/hash-tables/

1-1) Basic OP

  • get : get value from dict with default value if key not existed
1In [10]: d = {'a': 1, 'b': 2}
2    ...: d['a']
3Out[10]: 1
4
5In [11]: d.get('a')
6Out[11]: 1
7
8In [12]: d.get('c', 0)
9Out[12]: 0
10
11In [13]: d.get('z')
12
13In [14]:
  • setdefault()
    • https://www.w3schools.com/python/ref_dictionary_setdefault.asp
1#-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2# setdefault : will creatte key if key NOT existed (with value as well if defined)
3#-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4
5# syntax
6d.setdefault(new_key)
7d.setdefault(new_key, new_value)
8
9# 662 Maximum Width of Binary Tree
10car = {
11  "brand": "Ford",
12  "model": "Mustang",
13  "year": 1964
14}
15
16# example 1) insert key "my_key", since my_key not existed, -> make it as new key and value as None (since not defined)
17car.setdefault("my_key")
18print (car)
19# In [18]: car
20# Out[18]: {'brand': 'Ford', 'model': 'Mustang', 'year': 1964, 'my_key': None}
21
22# example 2) insert key "color", since my_key not existed, -> make it as new key and value as white
23car.setdefault("color", "white")
24print (car)
25# Out[22]:
26# {'brand': 'Ford',
27#  'model': 'Mustang',
28#  'year': 1964,
29#  'my_key': None,
30#  'color': 'white'}
  • Sort on hashmap (dict)
1# https://stackoverflow.com/questions/613183/how-do-i-sort-a-dictionary-by-value
2
3x = {1: 2, 3: 4, 4: 3, 2: 1, 0: 0}
4In [11]: x.items()
5Out[11]: dict_items([(1, 2), (3, 4), (4, 3), (2, 1), (0, 0)])
6
7#----------------------------------
8# Sort hashMap by key/value !!!
9#----------------------------------
10x = {1: 2, 3: 4, 4: 3, 2: 1, 0: 0}
11# note : have to use sorted(xxx, key=yyy), instead of xxx.sorted(....)
12### NOTE this !!! : x.items()
13sorted_x = sorted(x.items(), key=lambda kv: kv[1])
14print (sorted_x)
15# [(0, 0), (2, 1), (1, 2), (4, 3), (3, 4)]
16
17x = {1: 2, 3: 4, 4: 3, 2: 1, 0: 0}
18sorted_x = sorted(x.items(), key=lambda kv: kv[0])
19print (sorted_x)
20# [(0, 0), (1, 2), (2, 1), (3, 4), (4, 3)]
21
22# 451  Sort Characters By Frequency
23import collections
24class Solution(object):
25    def frequencySort(self, s):
26        count = collections.Counter(s)
27        count_dict = dict(count)
28        """
29        NOTE this !!!
30            1. use sorted()
31            2. count_dict.items()
32        """
33        count_tuple_sorted = sorted(count_dict.items(), key=lambda kv : -kv[1])
34        res = ''
35        for item in count_tuple_sorted:
36            res += item[0] * item[1]
37        return res

1# dict values -> array
2In [6]:
3   ...: mydict = {'a':['a1','a2','a3'], 'b':['b1','b2','b3']}
4   ...:
5   ...: res = [mydict[x] for x in mydict]
6   ...:
7   ...: print (res)
8[['a1', 'a2', 'a3'], ['b1', 'b2', 'b3']]
9
10# LC 049 Group Anagrams
11# V0
12# IDEA : HASH TABLE
13class Solution:
14    def groupAnagrams(self, strs):
15        res = {}
16        for item in strs:
17            k = ''.join(sorted(item))  # sort the string 
18            if k not in res:  #  check if exists in res 
19                res[k] = []
20            res[k].append(item)  # if same, put all the same string into dict k 
21        return [res[x] for x in res]  # output the result
  • Get max index for each element in a string
1s = 'ababcbacadefegdehijhklij'
2{k:v for k,v in enumerate(s)}
3
4# LC 763
5# V0
6# IDEA : GREEDY
7class Solution(object):
8    def partitionLabels(self, s):
9        d = {val:idx for idx, val in enumerate(list(s))}
10        #print (d)
11        res = []
12        tmp = set()
13        for idx, val in enumerate(s):
14            #print ("idx = " + str(idx) + " tmp = " + str(tmp) + "idx == d[val] = " + str(idx == d[val]))
15            """
16            ### have to fit 2 CONDITIONS so we can split the string
17            # -> 1) the element has "last time exist index" with current index
18            # -> 2) ALL of the elements in cache with "last time exist index" should <= current index
19            """
20            if idx == d[val] and all(idx >= d[t] for t in tmp):
21                res.append(idx+1)
22            else:
23                tmp.add(val)
24        _res = [res[0]] + [ res[i] - res[i-1] for i in range(1, len(res)) ]
25        return _res
26
27# V0'
28# IDEA : GREEDY
29class Solution(object):
30    def partitionLabels(self, S):
31        # note : this trick for get max index for each element in S
32        lindex = { c: i for i, c in enumerate(S) }
33        j = anchor = 0
34        ans = []
35        for i, c in enumerate(S):
36            ### NOTE : trick here
37            #          -> via below line of code, we can get the max idx of current substring which "has element only exist in itself"
38            #          -> e.g. the index we need to do partition 
39            j = max(j, lindex[c])
40            print ("i = " + str(i) + "," + " c = " + str(c) + "," +   " j = " + str(j) + "," +  " ans = " + str(ans))
41            if i == j:
42                ans.append(j - anchor + 1)
43                anchor = j + 1
44        return ans
  • Get pairs with specific sum
1# LC 1010 Pairs of Songs With Total Durations Divisible by 60
2d = {}
3res = 0
4DURATION = some_duration
5for num in nums:
6    tmp = num % DURATION # let's say sum is multiply by 60
7    ### NOTICE THIS :  (60 - tmp) % 60
8    if (DURATION - tmp) % DURATION in d:
9        res += d[(DURATION - tmp) % DURATION]
10    if tmp not in d:
11        d[tmp] = 1
12    else:
13        d[tmp] += 1
  • Get sub array sum
1# (algorithm book (labu) p.350)
2my_array = [1,2,3,4,5]
3my_array_pre = [0] * (len(my_array)+1)
4cur = 0
5for i in range(len(my_array)):
6    cur += my_array[i]
7    my_array_pre[i+1] += cur
8
9# In [17]: print ("my_array = " + str(my_array))
10#     ...: print ("my_array_pre = " + str(my_array_pre))
11# my_array = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
12# my_array_pre = [0, 1, 3, 6, 10, 15]
13
14#-----------------------------------------------
15# Get sub array sum !!!!!!!
16#    -> nums[i..j] sum = preSum[j+1] - preSum[i]
17#-----------------------------------------------
18
19# example 1 : sum of [1,2]
20my_array_pre[1+1] - my_array_pre[0]  # 1's index is 0, and 2's index is 1. (my_array = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
21
22# example 2 : sum of [2,3,4]
23my_array_pre[3+1] - my_array_pre[1] # 2's index is 1, and 4's index is 3. (my_array = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
  • Longest Substring
1# LC 003
2# 2 pointers + dict
3# ....
4l = 0
5d = {}
6res = 0
7for r in range(len(s)):
8    if s[r] in d:
9        l = max(l, d[s[r]]+1)
10    d[s[r]] = r
11    res = max(res, r-l+1)
12# ...

2) LC Example

2-1) Contiguous Array (LC 525)

Core Pattern: Transform + Prefix Sum + HashMap

Key Concept

Finding if there are at least 2 indexes with SAME count (running sum).

This is the same as finding any 2 x-axis with same y-axis in the visualization below.

Pattern Breakdown

1. Problem Transformation:

1Transform the binary array:
2- Treat 0 as -1
3- Treat 1 as +1
4
5Why? Equal 0s and 1s → sum of transformed array = 0

2. HashMap Structure:

1Map<Integer, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
2// {count: first_index_where_count_occurred}
3
4map.put(0, -1); // Initialize for subarrays starting at index 0

3. Core Logic:

1count: running sum (cumulative)
2  - +1 for each 1
3  - -1 for each 0
4
5If count(i) == count(j) where i < j:
6  → Elements between i and j sum to 0
7  → Subarray [i+1, j] is balanced (equal 0s and 1s)
8  → Length = j - i

4. Why Store FIRST Occurrence Only?

1To maximize length, we want the earliest index with this count.
2If count appears at indices [3, 7, 10]:
3  - Store index 3
4  - When we see count again at index 10, length = 10 - 3 = 7 (maximum)

5. Why Initialize map.put(0, -1)?

1If from index 0 to i, count = 0:
2  → Entire subarray [0, i] is balanced
3  → Length = i - (-1) = i + 1 ✓
4
5Without this initialization, we'd miss subarrays starting at index 0.

Visual Example

Sequence: [0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 1] Count progression: 0 → -1 → -2 → -3 → -4 → -3 → -2

The longest subarray has equal 0s and 1s when count returns to -2 (indices 1 and 5). Length = 5 - 1 = 4: [0, 1, 1, 1] wait, that’s wrong…

Actually, the charts show changes VS index of the sequence. We can find the longest subarray length is 4 (index 2 to 5), since index 2 and index 5 have the same count → count at index 2 and index 5 are the same (-2).

Mathematical Reasoning

Why Same Count Means Balanced Subarray:

1Let count(i) = cumulative sum at index i
2
3If count(i) == count(j) where i < j:
4  count(j) - count(i) = 0
5
6This means:
7  sum of elements from index (i+1) to j = 0
8
9In transformed array (0→-1, 1→+1):
10  sum = 0 means equal number of -1s and +1s
11  → equal number of 0s and 1s in original array

Implementation Template

1// Java Template
2public int findMaxLength(int[] nums) {
3    // Map: {count: first_index_where_count_occurred}
4    Map<Integer, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
5
6    // Initialize: handle subarrays starting at index 0
7    map.put(0, -1);
8
9    int maxLen = 0;
10    int count = 0;
11
12    for (int i = 0; i < nums.length; i++) {
13        // Transform: 0 → -1, 1 → +1
14        count += (nums[i] == 1) ? 1 : -1;
15
16        // If count seen before: calculate subarray length
17        if (map.containsKey(count)) {
18            maxLen = Math.max(maxLen, i - map.get(count));
19        } else {
20            // Store FIRST occurrence only (for max length)
21            map.put(count, i);
22        }
23    }
24
25    return maxLen;
26}

1# Python Template
2def findMaxLength(nums):
3    # Map: {count: first_index_where_count_occurred}
4    d = {0: -1}  # Initialize for subarrays starting at index 0
5
6    max_len = 0
7    count = 0
8
9    for i, num in enumerate(nums):
10        # Transform: 0 → -1, 1 → +1
11        count += 1 if num == 1 else -1
12
13        # If count seen before: calculate subarray length
14        if count in d:
15            max_len = max(max_len, i - d[count])
16        else:
17            # Store FIRST occurrence only (for max length)
18            d[count] = i
19
20    return max_len

Key Differences from LC 560 Pattern

Aspect LC 560 (Subarray Sum K) LC 525 (Contiguous Array)
Goal Count ALL subarrays Find LONGEST subarray
Map Value count (occurrences) index (first occurrence)
Map Update Always increment count Only if new count
Check Formula presum - k Same count
Initialization {0: 1} {0: -1}

Related Problems (Same Pattern)

  • LC 525: Contiguous Array (exactly this pattern)
  • LC 1124: Longest Well-Performing Interval (similar transformation)
  • LC 523: Continuous Subarray Sum (modulo transformation)
  • LC 325: Maximum Size Subarray Sum Equals k (prefix sum + index)
1# 525 Contiguous Array
2
3# V0
4# IDEA : HashMap
5#     -> SET UP A DICT,
6#     -> FIND MAX SUB ARRAY LENGH WHEN COUNT(0) == COUNT(1)
7#     -> (WHEN cur in _dict, THERE IS THE COUNT(0) == COUNT(1) CASE)
8# explaination : https://leetcode.com/problems/contiguous-array/discuss/99655/python-on-solution-with-visual-explanation
9class Solution(object):
10    def findMaxLength(self, nums):
11        # edge case
12        if len(nums) <= 1:
13            return 0
14        # note this edge case
15        if len(nums) == 2:
16            if nums.count(0) == nums.count(1):
17                return 2
18            else:
19                return 0
20
21        # NOTE !!! : init hash map like below (idx=0, no solution, for [0,1,1] case)
22        d = {0:-1} # {tmp_sum : index}
23        tmp = 0
24        res = 0
25        for k, v in enumerate(nums):
26            if v == 1:
27                tmp += 1
28            else:
29                tmp -= 1
30            """
31            Case 1 : if tmp sum in dict
32            # NOTE THIS : if tmp in d, return the max of (res,cur-index - index) from d with same cur-value
33            """
34            if tmp in d:
35                res = max(res, k - d[tmp])
36            """
37            Case 2 : if tmp sum NOT in dict
38            # NOTE THIS : if tmp not in d, then use its cur value as key, index as value
39            """
40            else:
41                d[tmp] = k ### NOTE : we just need to add index to dict at once, since what we need is MAX len of continous subarray with condition, so we only add 1st index to dist will make this work (max len subarray)
42        return res
43
44# V0'
45# https://github.com/yennanliu/CS_basics/blob/master/leetcode_python/Tree/contiguous-array.py
46# explanation : https://leetcode.com/problems/contiguous-array/discuss/99655/python-on-solution-with-visual-explanation
47# HASH MAP FIND EQUAL 0, 1
48class Solution(object):
49    def findMaxLength(self, nums):
50        r = 0
51        cur = 0
52        ### NOTE : WE HAVE TO INIT DICT LIKE BELOW
53        # https://blog.csdn.net/fuxuemingzhu/article/details/82667054
54        _dict = {0:-1}
55        for k, v in enumerate(nums):
56            if v == 1:
57                cur += 1
58            else:
59                cur -= 1
60            if cur in _dict:
61                r = max(r, k - _dict[cur])
62            else:
63                _dict[cur] = k
64        return r

2-1-1) Subarray Sums Divisible by K (LC 974)

Core Pattern: Prefix Sum + Modular Arithmetic + HashMap

Key Concept

Count ALL subarrays whose sum is divisible by K using remainder tracking.

If two prefix sums have the same remainder mod K, their difference is divisible by K.

Pattern Breakdown

1. Mathematical Foundation:

1If prefix[i] % k == prefix[j] % k  (where j < i)
2
3Then:
4  (prefix[i] - prefix[j]) % k == 0
5
6Which means:
7  prefix[i] - prefix[j] = sum of nums[j+1 .. i]
8
9Therefore:
10  The subarray [j+1, i] has a sum divisible by k

2. HashMap Structure:

1Map<Integer, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
2// {remainder: count}  ← Store COUNT, not index (similar to LC 560)
3
4map.put(0, 1); // Initialize for subarrays starting from beginning

3. Why Store Remainder COUNT (Not Index)?

1This is a "count ALL subarrays" problem (like LC 560).
2
3If remainder 3 appears at indices [2, 5, 8]:
4  - When we reach index 5: add 1 (subarray from index 2 to 5)
5  - When we reach index 8: add 2 (subarrays from 2→8 and 5→8)
6
7Total: 3 valid subarrays

4. Critical: Handle Negative Remainders

1int remainder = prefixSum % k;
2
3// MUST adjust negative remainders to positive
4if (remainder < 0) {
5    remainder += k;
6}
7
8// Or use this one-liner:
9remainder = ((prefixSum % k) + k) % k;

Why? In Java/Python, -7 % 5 = -2, but we need remainder 3 (since -2 ≡ 3 mod 5).

5. Initialization: Why map.put(0, 1)?

1If prefixSum % k == 0 at some index i:
2  → The entire subarray [0, i] is divisible by k
3  → We need to count this case
4
5Without initialization, we'd miss these subarrays.

Visual Example

Input: nums = [4, 5, 0, -2, -3, 1], k = 5

Prefix sums: [4, 9, 9, 7, 4, 5]

Remainders (mod 5): [4, 4, 4, 2, 4, 0]

Index Num PrefixSum Remainder Map State Count Added Total Count
- - 0 0 {0:1} - 0
0 4 4 4 {0:1, 4:1} 0 0
1 5 9 4 {0:1, 4:2} +1 1
2 0 9 4 {0:1, 4:3} +2 3
3 -2 7 2 {0:1, 4:3, 2:1} 0 3
4 -3 4 4 {0:1, 4:4, 2:1} +3 6
5 1 5 0 {0:2, 4:4, 2:1} +1 7

Result: 7 subarrays with sum divisible by 5

Subarrays found:

  1. [4,5,0,-2,-3,1] (entire array, remainder 0 at end)
  2. [5] (remainder 4 at indices 0 and 1)
  3. [5,0] (remainder 4 at indices 0 and 2)
  4. [5,0,-2,-3] (remainder 4 at indices 0 and 4)
  5. [0] (remainder 4 at indices 1 and 2)
  6. [0,-2,-3] (remainder 4 at indices 1 and 4)
  7. [-2,-3] (remainder 4 at indices 2 and 4)

Implementation Template

1// Java Template
2public int subarraysDivByK(int[] nums, int k) {
3    // Map: {remainder: count}
4    Map<Integer, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
5    map.put(0, 1); // Handle subarrays from beginning
6
7    int count = 0;
8    int prefixSum = 0;
9
10    for (int num : nums) {
11        prefixSum += num;
12
13        // Calculate remainder (handle negatives!)
14        int remainder = prefixSum % k;
15        if (remainder < 0) {
16            remainder += k;
17        }
18        // Or: int remainder = ((prefixSum % k) + k) % k;
19
20        // Add count of all previous same remainders
21        count += map.getOrDefault(remainder, 0);
22
23        // Update remainder count
24        map.put(remainder, map.getOrDefault(remainder, 0) + 1);
25    }
26
27    return count;
28}

1# Python Template
2def subarraysDivByK(nums, k):
3    # Map: {remainder: count}
4    remainder_count = {0: 1}
5
6    count = 0
7    prefix_sum = 0
8
9    for num in nums:
10        prefix_sum += num
11
12        # Calculate remainder (Python % handles negatives correctly)
13        remainder = prefix_sum % k
14
15        # Add count of all previous same remainders
16        count += remainder_count.get(remainder, 0)
17
18        # Update remainder count
19        remainder_count[remainder] = remainder_count.get(remainder, 0) + 1
20
21    return count

Note: Python’s % operator always returns positive remainders, so no adjustment needed.

Optimization: Array Instead of HashMap

Since remainders are always in range [0, k-1], use an array for better performance:

1public int subarraysDivByK(int[] nums, int k) {
2    int[] remainderCount = new int[k];
3    remainderCount[0] = 1;
4
5    int count = 0;
6    int prefixSum = 0;
7
8    for (int num : nums) {
9        prefixSum += num;
10        int remainder = ((prefixSum % k) + k) % k;
11
12        count += remainderCount[remainder];
13        remainderCount[remainder]++;
14    }
15
16    return count;
17}

Time Complexity: O(N) Space Complexity: O(K) instead of O(N)

Key Differences from Related Problems

Aspect LC 560 (Sum = K) LC 974 (Divisible by K) LC 525 (Equal 0/1)
Goal Count subarrays Count subarrays Find longest
Map Key prefixSum prefixSum % k count
Map Value count count first_index
Check Formula presum - k Same remainder Same count
Special Handling None Negative remainders! Transform 0→-1
Initialization {0: 1} {0: 1} {0: -1}

Critical: Why Negative Remainder Handling Matters

Example: nums = [-1, -2, -3], k = 5

Without adjustment:

1prefixSum = -1: remainder = -1 (wrong!)
2prefixSum = -3: remainder = -3 (wrong!)
3prefixSum = -6: remainder = -1 (wrong!)

With adjustment:

1prefixSum = -1: remainder = 4 (correct: -1 ≡ 4 mod 5)
2prefixSum = -3: remainder = 2 (correct: -3 ≡ 2 mod 5)
3prefixSum = -6: remainder = 4 (correct: -6 ≡ 4 mod 5)

Now remainders 4 match → subarray [-1] and [-2, -3] have the same remainder → subarray [-2, -3] has sum divisible by 5 ✓

Related Problems (Same Pattern)

  • LC 974: Subarray Sums Divisible by K (exactly this pattern)
  • LC 523: Continuous Subarray Sum (divisible, but length ≥ 2 constraint)
  • LC 560: Subarray Sum Equals K (no modulo, simpler)
  • LC 1248: Count Nice Subarrays (transform + count pattern)

2-1-2) Count Number of Nice Subarrays (LC 1248)

Core Pattern: Transform Odd Numbers → Prefix Sum Count (same as LC 560)

Key Concept

Count subarrays with exactly k odd numbers by treating each number as 0 (even) or 1 (odd), then applying the prefix sum + hashmap pattern.

Core Idea

Transform: Replace each element with num % 2 (1 if odd, 0 if even).

Now the problem becomes: count subarrays whose sum equals k — exactly LC 560!

1map: {oddCount: frequency}
2     → "How many times has this odd-count appeared so far?"
3
4At index i with current oddCount:
5  → Find how many previous positions had exactly (oddCount - k) odds
6  → Those form subarrays with exactly k odds ending at i

Why map.put(0, 1)?

1If oddCount == k at index i:
2  → Entire subarray [0, i] has exactly k odds
3  → oddCount - k = 0, must have {0: 1} pre-initialized

Implementation Template

1// Java - LC 1248
2public int numberOfSubarrays(int[] nums, int k) {
3    // map: {oddCount: frequency}
4    Map<Integer, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
5    map.put(0, 1);  // base case: 0 odds seen 1 time
6
7    int res = 0, oddCount = 0;
8
9    for (int num : nums) {
10        if (num % 2 == 1) oddCount++;  // treat odd as +1
11
12        // How many previous positions had (oddCount - k) odds?
13        res += map.getOrDefault(oddCount - k, 0);
14
15        // Update count AFTER checking (critical order!)
16        map.put(oddCount, map.getOrDefault(oddCount, 0) + 1);
17    }
18
19    return res;
20}

Alternative: Sliding Window (atMost trick)

1// Exactly k = atMost(k) - atMost(k-1)
2public int numberOfSubarrays(int[] nums, int k) {
3    return atMost(nums, k) - atMost(nums, k - 1);
4}
5
6private int atMost(int[] nums, int k) {
7    int l = 0, res = 0, oddCount = 0;
8    for (int r = 0; r < nums.length; r++) {
9        if (nums[r] % 2 == 1) oddCount++;
10        while (oddCount > k) {
11            if (nums[l] % 2 == 1) oddCount--;
12            l++;
13        }
14        res += (r - l + 1);
15    }
16    return res;
17}

Key Differences from Related Problems

Aspect LC 560 (Sum = K) LC 930 (Binary Sum = K) LC 1248 (Nice Subarrays)
Transform None (use values directly) Values are 0/1 already num % 2 → 0 or 1
Map Key prefixSum prefixSum oddCount
Map Value count count count
Init {0: 1} {0: 1} {0: 1}

Related Problems (Same Pattern)

  • LC 560: Subarray Sum Equals K (exact same pattern, no transform)
  • LC 930: Binary Subarrays with Sum (values are 0/1, same idea)
  • LC 974: Subarray Sums Divisible by K (modulo variant)
  • LC 1248: Count Nice Subarrays (this problem — transform to 0/1 then LC 560)

2-2) Continuous Subarray Sum

  • Similar concept as Contiguous Array (LC 525)
1# 523 Continuous Subarray Sum
2# V0
3# IDEA : HASH TABLE
4# -> if sum(nums[i:j]) % k == 0 for some i < j, 
5#   ->  then sum(nums[:j]) % k == sum(nums[:i]) % k  !!!!
6#   -> So we just need to use a dict to keep track of sum(nums[:i]) % k 
7#   -> and the corresponding index i. Once some later sum(nums[:i']) % k == sum(nums[:i]) % k and i' - i > 1, so we return True.
8class Solution(object):
9    def checkSubarraySum(self, nums, k):
10        """
11        # _dict = {0:-1} : for edge case (need to find a continuous subarray of size AT LEAST two )
12        # https://leetcode.com/problems/continuous-subarray-sum/discuss/236976/Python-solution
13        # 0: -1 is for edge case that current sum mod k == 0
14        # demo :
15                In [93]: nums = [0]
16                    ...: k = 1
17                    ...:
18                    ...:
19                    ...: s = Solution()
20                    ...: r = s.checkSubarraySum(nums, k)
21                    ...: print (r)
22                0
23                i - _dict[tmp] = 1
24                False
25        """
26        ### NOTE : we need to init _dict as {0:-1}
27        _dict = {0:-1}
28        tmp = 0
29        for i in range(len(nums)):
30            tmp += nums[i]
31            if k != 0:
32                ### NOTE : we get remainder of tmp by k
33                tmp = tmp % k
34            # if tmp in _dict, means there is the other sub part make sub array sum % k == 0
35            if tmp in _dict:
36                ### only if continuous sub array with length >= 2
37                if i - _dict[tmp] > 1:
38                    return True
39            else:
40                _dict[tmp] = i
41        return False

2-3) Group Anagrams

1# 049 Group Anagrams
2# V0
3# IDEA : HASH TABLE
4class Solution:
5    def groupAnagrams(self, strs):
6        res = {}
7        for item in strs:
8            k = ''.join(sorted(item))  # sort the string 
9            if k not in res:  #  check if exists in res 
10                res[k] = []
11            res[k].append(item)  # if same, put all the same string into dict k 
12        return [res[x] for x in res]  # output the result 
13

2-3) Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters

1# LC 003
2# V0'
3# IDEA : TWO POINTER + SLIDING WINDOW + DICT (NOTE this method !!!!)
4#       -> use a hash table (d) record visited "element" (e.g. : a,b,c,...)
5#          (but NOT sub-string)
6class Solution(object):
7    def lengthOfLongestSubstring(self, s):
8        d = {}
9        # left pointer
10        l = 0
11        res = 0
12        """
13        NOTE !!!
14
15        we move right pointer first, then left pointer
16        """
17        # NOTE !!! right pointer
18        for r in range(len(s)):
19            """
20            ### NOTE : deal with "s[r] in d" case ONLY !!! 
21            ### NOTE : if already visited, means "repeating"
22            #      -> then we need to update left pointer (l)
23            """
24            if s[r] in d:
25                """
26                NOTE !!! this
27                -> via max(l, d[s[r]] + 1) trick,
28                   we can get the "latest" idx of duplicated s[r], and start from that one
29                """
30                l = max(l, d[s[r]] + 1)
31            # if not visited yet, record the alphabet
32            # and re-calculate the max length
33            d[s[r]] = r
34            res = max(res, r -l + 1)
35        return res

2-4) Count Primes

1# LC 204 Count Primes
2# V0
3# IDEA : dict
4# https://leetcode.com/problems/count-primes/discuss/1343795/python%3A-sieve-of-eretosthenes
5# prime(x) : check if x is a prime
6# prime(0) = 0
7# prime(1) = 0
8# prime(2) = 0
9# prime(3) = 1
10# prime(4) = 2
11# prime(5) = 3
12# python 3
13class Solution:
14    def countPrimes(self, n):
15        # using sieve of eretosthenes algorithm
16        if n < 2: return 0
17        nonprimes = set()
18        for i in range(2, round(n**(1/2))+1):
19            if i not in nonprimes:
20                for j in range(i*i, n, i):
21                    nonprimes.add(j)
22        return n - len(nonprimes) - 2  # remove prime(1), prime(2)

2-5) Valid Sudoku

1# python
2# LC 036 Valid Sudoku
3# V0
4class Solution(object):
5    def isValidSudoku(self, board):
6        """
7        :type board: List[List[str]]
8        :rtype: bool
9        """
10        n = len(board)
11        return self.isValidRow(board) and self.isValidCol(board) and self.isValidNineCell(board)
12        
13    def isValidRow(self, board):
14        n = len(board)
15        for r in range(n):
16            row = [x for x in board[r] if x != '.']
17            if len(set(row)) != len(row): # if not repetition 
18                return False
19        return True
20
21    def isValidCol(self, board):
22        n = len(board)
23        for c in range(n):
24            col = [board[r][c] for r in range(n) if board[r][c] != '.']
25            if len(set(col)) != len(col): # if not repetition 
26                return False
27        return True
28
29    def isValidNineCell(self, board):
30        n = len(board)
31        for r in range(0, n, 3):
32            for c in range(0, n, 3):
33                cell = []
34                for i in range(3):
35                    for j in range(3):
36                        num = board[r + i][c + j]
37                        if num != '.':
38                            cell.append(num)
39                if len(set(cell)) != len(cell): # if not repetition 
40                    return False
41        return True

1// java
2// LC 036 Valid Sudoku
3// backtrack
4// (algorithm book (labu) p.311)
5boolean backtrack(char[][] boolean,int i, int j){
6
7    int m = 9, n = 9;
8    
9    if (j == n){
10        // if visit last col, start from next row
11        return backtrack(board, i + 1, 0);
12    }
13
14    if (i == m){
15        // found one solution, trigger base case
16        return true;
17    }
18
19    if (board[i][j] != '.'){
20        // if there id default number, then no need to looping
21        return backtrack(board, i, j + 1);
22    }
23
24    for (char ch = '1'; ch <= '9'; ch++){
25        // if there is no valid number, negelect it
26        if (!isValid(board, i, j, ch)){
27            continue;
28        }
29
30        board[i][j] = ch;
31
32        // if found one solution, return it and terminate the program
33        if (backtrack(board, i, j+1)){
34            return true;
35        }
36
37        board[i][j] = '.';
38    }
39
40    // if looping 1 ~ 9, still can't find a solution
41    // -> change a number to loop
42    return false;
43}
44
45bollean isValid(char[][] board, int r, int c, char n){
46    for (int i = 0; i < 9; i++){
47        // check if row has duplicate
48        if (board[r][i] == n) return false;
49        // check if col has duplicate
50        if (board[i][c] == n) return false;
51        // check if "3 x 3 matrix" has duplicate
52        if (board[ (r/3) * 3 + i / 3 ][ (c/3) * 3 + i % 3] == n) return false;
53    }
54    return true;
55}

2-6) Pairs of Songs With Total Durations Divisible by 60

1# LC 1010. Pairs of Songs With Total Durations Divisible by 60
2# V0
3# IDEA : dict
4# IDEA : NOTE : we only count "NUMBER OF PAIRS", instead get all pairs indexes
5class Solution(object):
6    def numPairsDivisibleBy60(self, time):
7        rem = {}
8        pairs = 0
9        for t in time:
10            #print ("rem = " + str(rem))
11            t %= 60
12            if (60 - t) % 60 in rem:
13                """
14                NOTE : this trick
15                -> we append "all 60 duration combinations count" via the existing times of element "(60 - t) % 60" 
16                """
17                pairs += rem[(60 - t) % 60]
18            if t not in rem:
19                rem[t] = 1
20            else:
21                ### NOTE : here : we plus 1 when an element already exist
22                rem[t] += 1
23        return pairs

2-7) Subarray Sum Equals K

1# LC 560 : Subarray Sum Equals K
2
3# V0
4# IDEA : HASH TABLE + sub array sum
5# IDEA : https://blog.csdn.net/fuxuemingzhu/article/details/82767119
6class Solution(object):
7    def subarraySum(self, nums, k):
8        n = len(nums)
9        d = collections.defaultdict(int)
10        d[0] = 1
11        sum = 0
12        res = 0
13        for i in range(n):
14            sum += nums[i]
15            # if sum - k in d
16            #  -> if sum - (every _ in d) == k
17            if sum - k in d:
18                res += d[sum - k]
19            d[sum] += 1
20        return res
21
22# V0'
23# IDEA : HASH TABLE + sub array sum
24class Solution:
25    def subarraySum(self, nums, k):
26        # write your code here
27        for i in range(1, len(nums)):
28            nums[i] += nums[i - 1]
29        print ("nums = " + str(nums))
30        d = {0:1}
31        ans = 0
32        for i in range(len(nums)):
33            # check sub array equals k
34            if(d.get(nums[i] - k) != None):
35                ans += d[nums[i] - k]
36            # update dict
37            if nums[i] not in d:
38                d[nums[i]] = 1
39            else:
40                d[nums[i]] += 1
41        return ans

1// LC 560 : Subarray Sum Equals K
2// java
3// (algorithm book (labu) p.350)
4// V1 : brute force + cum sum
5int subarraySum(int[] nums, int k){
6    int n = nums.length;
7    // init pre sum
8    int[] sum = new int[n+1];
9    sum[0] = 0;
10    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++){
11        sum[i+1] = sum[i] + nums[i];
12    }
13
14    int ans = 0;
15    // loop over all sub array
16    for (int i=1; i <= n; i++){
17        for (int j=0; j < i; j++){
18            // sum of nums[j...i-1]
19            if (sum[i] - sum[j] == k){
20                ans += 1;
21            }
22        }
23    }
24    return ans;
25}
26
27// (algorithm book (labu) p.350)
28// V2 : hash map + cum sum
29int subarraySum(int[] nums, int k){
30    int n = nums.length;
31    // map :  key : prefix, value : prefix exists count
32    // init hash map
33    HashMap<Integer, Integer> preSum = new HashMap<Integer, Integer>();
34
35    // base case
36    preSum.put(0,1);
37
38    int ans = 0;
39    sum0_i = 0;
40
41    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++){
42        sum0_i += nums[i];
43        // for presum : nums[0..j]
44        int sum0_j = sum0_i - k;
45        // if there is already presum, update the ans directly
46        if (preSum.containsKey(sum0_j)){
47            ans += preSum.get(sum0_j);
48        }
49        // add prefix and nums[0..i] and record exists count
50        preSum.put(sum0_i, preSum.getOrDefault(sum0_i,0) + 1);
51    }
52    return ans;
53}

2-8) K-diff Pairs in an Array

1# LC 532 K-diff Pairs in an Array
2# V0
3# IDEA : HASH TABLE
4import collections
5class Solution(object):
6    def findPairs(self, nums, k):
7        answer = 0
8        cnt = collections.Counter(nums)
9        # NOTE THIS : !!! we use set(nums) for reduced time complexity, and deal with k == 0 case separately
10        for num in set(nums):
11            """
12            # [b - a] = k
13            #  -> b - a = +k or -k
14            #  -> b = k + a or b = -k + a
15            #  -> however, 0 <= k <= 10^7, so ONLY b = k + a is possible
16
17            2 cases
18                -> case 1) k > 0 and num + k in cnt
19                -> case 2) k == 0 and cnt[num] > 1
20            """
21            # case 1) k > 0 and num + k in cnt
22            if k > 0 and num + k in cnt: # | a - b | = k -> a - b = +k or -k, but here don't have to deal with "a - b = -k" case, since this sutuation will be covered when go through whole nums  
23                answer += 1
24            # case 2) k == 0 and cnt[num] > 1
25            if k == 0 and cnt[num] > 1:  # for cases k = 0 ->  pair like (1,1) will work. (i.e. 1 + (-1))
26                answer += 1
27        return answer
28
29# V0'
30# IDEA : SORT + BRUTE FORCE + BREAK
31class Solution(object):
32    def findPairs(self, nums, k):
33        # edge case
34        if not nums and k:
35            return 0
36        nums.sort()
37        res = 0
38        tmp = []
39        for i in range(len(nums)):
40            for j in range(i+1, len(nums)):
41                if abs(nums[j] - nums[i]) == k:
42                    cur = [nums[i], nums[j]]
43                    cur.sort()
44                    if cur not in tmp:
45                        res += 1
46                        tmp.append(cur)
47                elif abs(nums[j] - nums[i]) > k:
48                    break
49        return res

2-9) Sentence Similarity

1# LC 734. Sentence Similarity
2# V0'
3# https://zxi.mytechroad.com/blog/hashtable/leetcode-734-sentence-similarity/
4import collections
5class Solution(object):
6    def areSentencesSimilar(self, words1, words2, pairs):
7        if len(words1) != len(words2): return False
8        similars = collections.defaultdict(set)
9        for w1, w2 in pairs:
10            similars[w1].add(w2)
11            similars[w2].add(w1)
12        for w1, w2 in zip(words1, words2):
13            if w1 != w2 and w2 not in similars[w1]:
14                return False
15        return True
16
17# V0
18# IDEA : array op
19#   -> Apart from edge cases
20#   -> there are cases we need to consider
21#     -> 1) if sentence1[i] == sentence2[i]
22#     -> 2) if sentence1[i] != sentence2[i] and
23#           -> [sentence1[i], sentence2[i]] in similarPairs
24#           -> [sentence2[i], sentence1[i]] in similarPairs
25class Solution(object):
26    def areSentencesSimilar(self, sentence1, sentence2, similarPairs):
27        # edge case
28        if sentence1 == sentence2:
29            return True
30        if len(sentence1) != len(sentence2):
31            return False
32        for i in range(len(sentence1)):
33            tmp = [sentence1[i], sentence2[i]]
34            """
35            NOTE : below condition
36                1) sentence1[i] != sentence2[i]
37                  AND
38                2) (tmp not in similarPairs and tmp[::-1] not in similarPairs)
39
40                -> return false
41            """
42            if sentence1[i] != sentence2[i] and (tmp not in similarPairs and tmp[::-1] not in similarPairs):
43                return False
44        return True

2-10) LRU Cache

1# LC 146 LRU Cache
2# note : there is also array/queue approach
3# V1
4# IDEA : Ordered dictionary
5# https://leetcode.com/problems/lru-cache/solution/
6# IDEA : 
7#       -> There is a structure called ordered dictionary, it combines behind both hashmap and linked list. 
8#       -> In Python this structure is called OrderedDict 
9#       -> and in Java LinkedHashMap.
10from collections import OrderedDict
11class LRUCache(OrderedDict):
12
13    def __init__(self, capacity):
14        """
15        :type capacity: int
16        """
17        self.capacity = capacity
18
19    def get(self, key):
20        """
21        :type key: int
22        :rtype: int
23        """
24        if key not in self:
25            return - 1
26        
27        self.move_to_end(key)
28        return self[key]
29
30    def put(self, key, value):
31        """
32        :type key: int
33        :type value: int
34        :rtype: void
35        """
36        if key in self:
37            self.move_to_end(key)
38        self[key] = value
39        if len(self) > self.capacity:
40            self.popitem(last = False)

2-11) Find All Anagrams in a String

1# LC 438. Find All Anagrams in a String
2# V0
3# IDEA : SLIDING WINDOW + collections.Counter()
4class Solution(object):
5    def findAnagrams(self, s, p):
6        ls, lp = len(s), len(p)
7        cp = collections.Counter(p)
8        cs = collections.Counter()
9        ans = []
10        for i in range(ls):
11            cs[s[i]] += 1
12            if i >= lp:
13                cs[s[i - lp]] -= 1
14                ### BE AWARE OF IT
15                if cs[s[i - lp]] == 0:
16                    del cs[s[i - lp]]
17            if cs == cp:
18                ans.append(i - lp + 1)
19        return ans

2-12) Brick Wall

1# LC 554. Brick Wall
2# V0
3# IDEA : HASH TABLE + COUNTER UPDATE (looping every element in the list and cumsum and 
4import collections
5class Solution(object):
6    def leastBricks(self, wall):
7        _counter = collections.Counter()
8        count = 0
9        # go through every sub-wall in wall
10        for w in wall:
11            cum_sum = 0
12            # go through every element in sub-wall
13            for i in range(len(w) - 1):
14                cum_sum += w[i]
15                ### NOTE we can update collections.Counter() via below
16                _counter.update([cum_sum])
17                count = max(count, _counter[cum_sum])
18        return len(wall) - count

2-13) Maximum Size Subarray Sum Equals k

1// java
2// LC 325
3    public int maxSubArrayLen_0_1(int[] nums, int k) {
4        // Map to store (prefixSum, index)
5        Map<Integer, Integer> preSumMap = new HashMap<>();
6        preSumMap.put(0, -1); // Initialize for subarrays starting from index 0
7
8        int curSum = 0;
9        int maxSize = 0;
10
11        for (int i = 0; i < nums.length; i++) {
12            curSum += nums[i];
13
14            /**
15             *
16             *   TODO: check if `preSum == k` already existed before (within loop over nunms)
17             *
18             *    -> if preSum == k existed
19             *    -> (let's say current idx = j, and a previous idx = i, can make sum(i, j) == k)
20             *    -> `preSum(j) - preSum(i) = k`  !!!!
21             *      -> preSum(j) if what we have  (preSum)
22             *      ----> so need to check if `preSum(j) - k` exists in map  !!!
23             */
24            // Check if there's a prefix sum such that curSum - prefixSum = k
25            /**
26             *  Prefix sum
27             *
28             *
29             * The prefix sum approach works because any subarray sum can be expressed in terms of two prefix sums:
30             *
31             *
32             * sum of subarray[i,j] = prefixSum[j] - prefixSum[i-1]
33             *
34             *
35             * Where:
36             *  •   prefixSum[j] is the cumulative sum of the array up to index j.
37             *  •   prefixSum[i-1] is the cumulative sum of the array up to index i-1.
38             *
39             * Rewriting this:
40             *
41             * -> prefixSum[j] - prefixSum[i-1] = k
42             *
43             * -> prefixSum[i-1] = prefixSum[j] - k
44             *
45             *
46             * Thus, the task is to find a previous prefix
47             * sum (prefixSum[i-1]) such that the
48             * difference between the current
49             * prefix sum (prefixSum[j]) and that value equals k.
50             *
51             *
52             *
53             *  How the Code Works
54             *
55             *  1.  Tracking Prefix Sums:
56             *      •   curSum is the cumulative prefix sum up to the current index i.
57             *      •   The map preSumMap stores previously seen prefix sums as keys, with their earliest index as the value.
58             *  2.  Checking for Subarrays:
59             *      •   At any index i, the condition curSum - k checks if there exists a previously seen prefix sum that, when subtracted from the current cumulative sum, gives the desired subarray sum k.
60             *
61             *  3.  Why It Covers All Possible Subarrays******:
62             *      •   The map contains all prefix sums seen so far, so it inherently includes all potential starting points of subarrays.
63             *      •   If a subarray [start, i] has a sum equal to k, the difference curSum - k corresponds to the prefix sum at start - 1. Since the map stores all previously seen prefix sums, this difference is guaranteed to be checked.
64             *
65             */
66            if (preSumMap.containsKey(curSum - k)) {
67                maxSize = Math.max(maxSize, i - preSumMap.get(curSum - k));
68            }
69
70            // Add current prefix sum to the map if not already present
71            preSumMap.putIfAbsent(curSum, i);
72        }
73
74        return maxSize;
75    }

2-14) Smallest Common Region

1// java
2// LC 1257
3
4// V0-1
5// IDEA: HASHMAP (fixed by gpt)
6// TODO: validate
7public String findSmallestRegion_0_1(List<List<String>> regions, String region1, String region2) {
8
9    // Map each region to its parent
10    /**
11     *  NOTE !!!
12     *
13     *   map : {child : parent}
14     *
15     *   -> so the key is child, and the value is its parent
16     *
17     */
18    Map<String, String> parentMap = new HashMap<>();
19
20    for (List<String> regionList : regions) {
21        String parent = regionList.get(0);
22        for (int i = 1; i < regionList.size(); i++) {
23            parentMap.put(regionList.get(i), parent);
24        }
25    }
26
27    // Track ancestors of region1
28    /**  NOTE !!!
29     *
30     *  we use `set` to track `parents` (ancestors)
31     *  if exists, add it to set,
32     *  and set `current region` as its `parent`
33     *
34     */
35    Set<String> ancestors = new HashSet<>();
36    while (region1 != null) {
37        ancestors.add(region1);
38        region1 = parentMap.get(region1);
39    }
40
41    // Traverse region2’s ancestors until we find one in region1’s ancestor set
42    while (!ancestors.contains(region2)) {
43        region2 = parentMap.get(region2);
44    }
45
46    return region2;
47}

Decision Framework

Pattern Selection Flowchart

1START: Analyzing Hash Map Problem
2                   |
3                   v
4           Are you counting elements/frequency?
5                   |
6                YES|    NO
7                   |     |
8                   v     v
9         [CATEGORY 1:     Looking for pairs/complements?
10          COUNTING]            |
11                            YES|    NO
12                               |     |
13                               v     v
14                     [CATEGORY 2:    Need to find subarray properties?
15                      TWO SUM]            |
16                                       YES|    NO
17                                          |     |
18                                          v     v
19                                 [CATEGORY 3:    Using sliding window technique?
20                                  PREFIX SUM]          |
21                                                    YES|    NO
22                                                       |     |
23                                                       v     v
24                                             [CATEGORY 4:    Designing a data structure?
25                                              SLIDING]            |
26                                                               YES|    NO
27                                                                  |     |
28                                                                  v     v
29                                                        [CATEGORY 5:    Working with graphs/trees?
30                                                         DESIGN]              |
31                                                                           YES|    NO
32                                                                              |     |
33                                                                              v     v
34                                                                    [CATEGORY 6:   [OTHER PATTERNS]
35                                                                     GRAPH]

Decision Questions

  1. Counting Problems:

    • “Do I need to track frequency of elements?”
    • “Am I looking for duplicates or unique elements?”
    • “Do I need to sort by frequency?”

  2. Two Sum Variants:

    • “Am I looking for pairs that sum to a target?”
    • “Do I need indices or just existence?”
    • “Are there multiple valid pairs?”

  3. Prefix Sum Problems:

    • “Do I need subarray sum information?”
    • “Can I transform this to prefix sum lookup?”
    • “Am I looking for subarrays with specific properties?”

  4. Sliding Window:

    • “Do I need a dynamic window of elements?”
    • “Am I tracking state within a window?”
    • “Does window size change based on conditions?”

  5. Design Problems:

    • “Do I need to implement get/put operations?”
    • “Are there capacity or eviction requirements?”
    • “Do I need O(1) average operations?”

  6. Graph/Tree Problems:

    • “Am I dealing with node relationships?”
    • “Do I need to map nodes during traversal?”
    • “Are there parent-child or neighbor relationships?”

Time Complexity Guide

Pattern Average Case Worst Case Space When to Use
Counting O(n) O(n) O(n) Frequency analysis
Two Sum O(n) O(n) O(n) Finding pairs/complements
Prefix Sum O(n) O(n) O(n) Subarray problems
Sliding Window O(n) O(n) O(k) Dynamic windows
Design/Cache O(1)* O(n) O(n) Data structure design
Graph/Tree O(n) O(n) O(n) Node relationship tracking

*Amortized for most cache operations

Interview Tips and Best Practices

🎯 Quick Recognition Patterns

If you see… Think… Pattern
“count frequency” Counting/Frequency Template 1
“find pair”, “target sum” Two Sum Template 2
“subarray sum equals” Prefix Sum Template 3
“substring without repeating” Sliding Window Template 4
“implement cache” Design/Cache Template 5
“clone graph”, “tree paths” Graph/Tree Template 6

💡 Key Insights to Remember

  1. Space-Time Tradeoff: Hash maps trade extra O(n) space for O(1) average lookup time
  2. Prefix Sum Magic: subarray[i,j] = prefixSum[j] - prefixSum[i-1]
  3. Sliding Window State: Use hash map to maintain window properties efficiently
  4. Complement Thinking: Instead of checking all pairs, store elements and check complements
  5. Index vs Value: Decide whether to store indices, values, or both as hash map values
  6. Frequency Counting: Most string/array problems can be solved with frequency analysis

🔧 Implementation Best Practices

Python Best Practices

1# 1. Use defaultdict for cleaner counting code
2from collections import defaultdict
3count = defaultdict(int)  # No need for get(key, 0)
4
5# 2. Use Counter for frequency problems
6from collections import Counter
7freq = Counter(arr)  # Automatically counts frequencies
8
9# 3. Handle edge cases with dict.get()
10value = my_dict.get(key, default_value)
11
12# 4. Clean up zero counts to save space
13if count[key] == 0:
14    del count[key]
15
16# 5. Use enumerate when you need both index and value
17for i, val in enumerate(arr):
18    # Use both i and val

Java Best Practices

1// 1. Use getOrDefault to avoid null checks
2map.put(key, map.getOrDefault(key, 0) + 1);
3
4// 2. Use containsKey for existence checks
5if (map.containsKey(key)) { /* ... */ }
6
7// 3. Initialize with appropriate capacity
8Map<String, Integer> map = new HashMap<>(expectedSize);
9
10// 4. Use putIfAbsent for first occurrence
11map.putIfAbsent(key, index);  // Only puts if key doesn't exist

⚠️ Common Mistakes to Avoid

  1. Hash Collision Assumption: Remember that worst-case time complexity is O(n), not O(1)

  2. Index Out of Bounds:

    1# Wrong: Can cause index errors
2if target - nums[i] in seen:
3    return [i, seen[target - nums[i]]]
4seen[nums[i]] = i
5
6# Right: Check existence first
7if target - nums[i] in seen:
8    return [seen[target - nums[i]], i]
9seen[nums[i]] = i

  3. Modifying Dict During Iteration:

    1# Wrong: Can cause runtime errors
2for key in my_dict:
3    if condition:
4        del my_dict[key]
5
6# Right: Collect keys first
7to_delete = [k for k, v in my_dict.items() if condition]
8for k in to_delete:
9    del my_dict[k]

  4. Ignoring Edge Cases:

    • Empty input arrays
    • Single element arrays
    • All elements the same
    • Target not achievable

  5. Wrong Data Structure Choice:

    • Use set() for existence checks only
    • Use dict() when you need key-value mapping
    • Use Counter() for frequency counting

🏆 Advanced Techniques

1. Multiple Hash Maps

1# Track multiple relationships simultaneously
2def complex_problem(arr):
3    index_map = {}      # value -> index
4    freq_map = {}       # value -> frequency
5    reverse_map = {}    # index -> value
6    
7    for i, val in enumerate(arr):
8        index_map[val] = i
9        freq_map[val] = freq_map.get(val, 0) + 1
10        reverse_map[i] = val

2. Hash Map + Other Data Structures

1# Hash Map + Priority Queue (Heap)
2import heapq
3from collections import defaultdict
4
5def top_k_frequent(nums, k):
6    count = defaultdict(int)
7    for num in nums:
8        count[num] += 1
9    
10    # Use heap with frequency
11    heap = []
12    for num, freq in count.items():
13        heapq.heappush(heap, (-freq, num))  # Max heap using negative values
14    
15    result = []
16    for _ in range(k):
17        result.append(heapq.heappop(heap)[1])
18    return result

3. Rolling Hash for String Problems

1# For substring pattern matching
2def rolling_hash_example(s, pattern):
3    base, mod = 256, 10**9 + 7
4    pattern_hash = sum(ord(c) * pow(base, i, mod) for i, c in enumerate(pattern)) % mod
5    
6    # Slide window and update hash incrementally
7    # ... implementation details

📈 Performance Optimization

  1. Choose Right Hash Function: Python’s built-in hash is usually optimal
  2. Avoid Unnecessary Rehashing: Pre-size maps when possible
  3. Memory Cleanup: Remove zero-count entries in frequency maps
  4. Use Appropriate Load Factor: Default 0.75 is usually optimal

🎯 Interview Preparation Checklist

  • [ ] Master all 6 templates and when to use each
  • [ ] Practice 3-5 problems from each category
  • [ ] Understand time/space complexity for each pattern
  • [ ] Know common edge cases and how to handle them
  • [ ] Practice explaining hash collision resolution
  • [ ] Be comfortable with both Python dict and Java HashMap APIs
  • [ ] Understand when NOT to use hash maps (sorted data, range queries, etc.)

📚 Summary

Hash maps are one of the most versatile data structures in competitive programming and technical interviews. The key to mastering hash map problems is:

  1. Pattern Recognition: Quickly identify which of the 6 categories a problem falls into
  2. Template Application: Use the appropriate template as a starting point
  3. Edge Case Handling: Always consider empty inputs, duplicates, and boundary conditions
  4. Complexity Analysis: Understand both average and worst-case performance
  5. Code Clarity: Write clean, readable code with proper variable names

Remember: Hash maps excel at problems requiring fast lookups, frequency counting, and avoiding nested loops. When you see O(n²) brute force solutions, ask yourself: “Can I use a hash map to store some information and reduce this to O(n)?”
